_comment: Skill Graph rebuild Phase 3 (2026-07-23) — canonical B2 curriculum. Sourced from the standard CEFR progression, sequenced the way mainstream coursebooks order B2 topics — informed by their structure, not copied text. Cross-links back to real a1.json/a2.json/b1.json keys where B2 genuinely builds on an earlier foundation. Consumed by POST /api/admin/skill-graph/nodes/import.

nodes.title: Mixed conditionals
nodes.description: Combining a past condition with a present result, or vice versa.
nodes.descriptionForAi: Cross-level: combines B1's second and third conditionals.

nodes.title: Wish and if only
nodes.description: Expressing regrets and wishes about the present, past, and future.

nodes.title: Future perfect
nodes.description: Describing an action that will be completed before a future point.

nodes.title: Non-defining relative clauses
nodes.description: Adding extra, non-essential information set off by commas.

nodes.title: Advanced reporting verbs
nodes.description: Using precise reporting verbs (suggest, deny, admit, insist) instead of just said/told.

nodes.title: Passive voice: advanced tenses
nodes.description: Using the passive with perfect, continuous, and modal forms.

nodes.title: Modals of criticism (should have, needn't have)
nodes.description: Criticizing or evaluating a past action that can't be changed.

nodes.title: Inversion for emphasis
nodes.description: Fronting a negative adverbial for dramatic/formal emphasis (Never have I seen...).

nodes.title: Cleft sentences
nodes.description: Rearranging a sentence to isolate and emphasize a specific point.

nodes.title: Participle clauses
nodes.description: Using -ing/-ed clauses to make sentences more concise.

nodes.title: Emphatic structures with do/does/did
nodes.description: Adding emphatic do for contrast or insistence (I DO like it).

nodes.title: Adverbial clauses of concession
nodes.description: Formally linking contrasting ideas (even though, whereas, while).

nodes.title: Adverbial clauses of reason and purpose
nodes.description: Formally linking cause/reason and intended purpose (given that, so as to).

nodes.title: Introduction to the subjunctive mood
nodes.description: Formal expressions of demand/suggestion (It is vital that he be present).

nodes.title: Ellipsis and substitution
nodes.description: Dropping or replacing words to avoid repetition like a native speaker.

nodes.title: Advanced article usage
nodes.description: Subtle article choices in generalizations and abstract nouns.

nodes.title: Advanced three-part phrasal verbs
nodes.description: Using less common three-word phrasal verbs (look forward to, put up with).

nodes.title: Would rather / it's time + past
nodes.description: Expressing preference and suggestion with unusual tense shifts.

nodes.title: Despite/in spite of vs. although
nodes.description: Choosing correctly between preposition + noun/gerund and conjunction + clause for contrast.

nodes.title: Advanced question tag nuance
nodes.description: Using question tags for rhetorical effect, not just confirmation.

nodes.title: Hedging language for softening claims
nodes.description: Softening statements with modal/adverbial hedges (it seems, arguably, tends to).

nodes.title: Business and management
nodes.description: Vocabulary for business strategy, management, and corporate life.

nodes.title: Media and journalism
nodes.description: Vocabulary for discussing news media and journalism.

nodes.title: General academic vocabulary
nodes.description: High-frequency academic words used across subjects.

nodes.title: Advanced idioms
nodes.description: Less common idioms used in more sophisticated conversation.

nodes.title: Business phrasal verbs
nodes.description: Phrasal verbs common in meetings and workplace writing.

nodes.title: Psychology and behavior
nodes.description: Vocabulary for discussing motivation, behavior, and mental processes.

nodes.title: Politics and government
nodes.description: Vocabulary for discussing political systems and current affairs.

nodes.title: Health and wellbeing (advanced)
nodes.description: Vocabulary for mental health, wellbeing, and lifestyle.

nodes.title: Technology and artificial intelligence
nodes.description: Vocabulary for discussing modern technology and AI's impact on society.

nodes.title: Describing statistics precisely
nodes.description: More precise data-description vocabulary (a slight/dramatic/steady rise).

nodes.title: Formal register vocabulary
nodes.description: Formal synonyms for everyday words used in official contexts.

nodes.title: Collocations for academic writing
nodes.description: Fixed word pairings common in academic prose.

nodes.title: Negotiation and persuasion language
nodes.description: Vocabulary for negotiating and persuading in professional contexts.

nodes.title: Environmental sustainability
nodes.description: Vocabulary for sustainability, renewable energy, and conservation.

nodes.title: Legal and official language
nodes.description: Vocabulary for understanding contracts, forms, and legal notices.

nodes.title: Emotional intelligence vocabulary
nodes.description: Nuanced vocabulary for describing empathy, self-awareness, and emotional regulation.

nodes.title: Travel and cultural immersion
nodes.description: Vocabulary for deeper cultural experiences while travelling or relocating.

nodes.title: Abstract concepts (freedom, justice, equality)
nodes.description: Vocabulary for discussing abstract social and philosophical concepts.

nodes.title: Workplace conflict resolution
nodes.description: Vocabulary for describing and resolving disagreements at work.

nodes.title: Euphemisms and polite language
nodes.description: Softer, indirect ways of referring to sensitive topics.

nodes.title: Reading an advanced editorial or opinion piece
nodes.description: Following a well-developed written argument on a current issue.

nodes.title: Reading an academic-style article
nodes.description: Understanding a text written in a more formal, academic register.

nodes.title: Identifying bias and tone
nodes.description: Recognizing a text's underlying bias and emotional tone.

nodes.title: Understanding implied meaning
nodes.description: Reading between the lines to grasp meaning not stated directly.

nodes.title: Reading a business report or proposal
nodes.description: Understanding the structure and content of a formal business document.

nodes.title: Reading a literary extract with figurative language
nodes.description: Understanding metaphor, simile, and descriptive imagery in literature.

nodes.title: Contrasting viewpoints in two texts
nodes.description: Comparing two texts that argue opposing positions on the same topic.

nodes.title: Following a complex, multi-step argument
nodes.description: Tracking a longer written argument with several supporting stages.

nodes.title: Understanding statistics in a report
nodes.description: Interpreting precisely-described numerical data in a written report.

nodes.title: Recognizing register (formal vs. informal, advanced)
nodes.description: Distinguishing subtle register differences across a range of text types.

nodes.title: Understanding a formal presentation
nodes.description: Following a longer, structured spoken presentation.

nodes.title: Understanding fast, natural speech
nodes.description: Following speech at a natural, unslowed conversational pace.

nodes.title: Identifying implied criticism or sarcasm
nodes.description: Recognizing sarcasm and indirect criticism from tone and word choice.

nodes.title: Understanding a panel discussion
nodes.description: Following multiple speakers exchanging views quickly.

nodes.title: Following complex spoken instructions
nodes.description: Understanding multi-step spoken instructions without repetition.

nodes.title: Understanding news interviews
nodes.description: Following a more formal, fast-paced broadcast interview.

nodes.title: Recognizing a speaker's attitude changing
nodes.description: Noticing when a speaker's opinion or tone shifts during a recording.

nodes.title: Understanding academic lectures
nodes.description: Following a longer academic-style lecture on a specialized topic.

nodes.title: Understanding idiomatic spoken English
nodes.description: Following natural speech full of idioms and colloquial expressions.

nodes.title: Summarizing key points after listening
nodes.description: Producing an accurate summary immediately after a recording ends.

nodes.title: Writing a formal report
nodes.description: Writing a structured formal report with sections and recommendations.

nodes.title: Writing a persuasive essay
nodes.description: Building a one-sided persuasive argument with rhetorical technique.

nodes.title: Writing a formal proposal
nodes.description: Proposing a plan or idea formally, with clearly stated purpose and reasons.

nodes.title: Writing with appropriate register
nodes.description: Matching vocabulary and tone to a formal or informal writing task.

nodes.title: Writing a review with balanced criticism
nodes.description: Writing a review that fairly presents both strengths and weaknesses.

nodes.title: Writing a discursive essay
nodes.description: Presenting multiple viewpoints on an issue before giving a balanced conclusion.

nodes.title: Summarizing multiple sources
nodes.description: Synthesizing information from more than one text into one summary.

nodes.title: Writing an advanced cover letter
nodes.description: Writing a persuasive, well-targeted cover letter for a specific role.

nodes.title: Editing for conciseness
nodes.description: Cutting unnecessary words and restructuring for a tighter, clearer sentence.

nodes.title: Writing using hedging language
nodes.description: Softening claims appropriately in academic/professional writing.

nodes.title: Debating a controversial topic
nodes.description: Arguing a position on a genuinely controversial issue.

nodes.title: Giving a structured presentation
nodes.description: Delivering a longer presentation with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion.

nodes.title: Negotiating a business deal
nodes.description: A roleplay negotiating terms in a professional context.

nodes.title: Expressing nuanced opinions
nodes.description: Giving a carefully qualified opinion rather than a blunt statement.

nodes.title: Discussing hypothetical past regrets
nodes.description: Talking about how things might have been different, using mixed conditionals.

nodes.title: Criticizing and evaluating past decisions
nodes.description: Evaluating what should/shouldn't have been done differently.

nodes.title: Participating in a panel discussion
nodes.description: Contributing your view among several other speakers.

nodes.title: Resolving a workplace conflict
nodes.description: A roleplay mediating or resolving a disagreement at work.

nodes.title: Discussing ethical dilemmas
nodes.description: Reasoning through a moral dilemma and justifying a position.

nodes.title: Giving constructive feedback
nodes.description: Delivering critical feedback in a supportive, professional way.

nodes.title: Discussing technology and society
nodes.description: Talking about the impact of technology and AI on everyday life.

nodes.title: Using persuasive rhetoric
nodes.description: Using rhetorical techniques (repetition, rhetorical questions) to persuade.

nodes.title: Stress shift in related word forms
nodes.description: Noticing how stress moves between noun/verb/adjective forms of a word.

nodes.title: Intonation for sarcasm and irony
nodes.description: Recognizing/producing the intonation patterns typical of sarcasm.

nodes.title: Advanced connected speech: assimilation
nodes.description: Recognizing/producing sound changes where adjacent sounds influence each other.

nodes.title: Prosody in extended speech
nodes.description: Maintaining natural rhythm and intonation over a longer spoken turn.

nodes.title: Reducing first-language accent features
nodes.description: Identifying and working on personal pronunciation habits from the L1.

nodes.title: Emphatic stress for contrast
nodes.description: Stressing an emphatic auxiliary correctly (I DO agree, but...).

nodes.title: Intonation in formal presentations
nodes.description: Using clear, structured intonation patterns typical of formal presenting.

nodes.title: Speaking spontaneously on abstract topics
nodes.description: Responding fluently to an abstract question with no preparation time.

nodes.title: Using advanced discourse markers
nodes.description: Using a wider range of markers (having said that, mind you, as it happens).

nodes.title: Self-correcting register mismatches
nodes.description: Noticing and adjusting when your tone doesn't match the situation.

nodes.title: Balancing fluency and precision
nodes.description: Deciding in real time when speed matters more than perfect accuracy.

nodes.title: Handling interruptions smoothly
nodes.description: Continuing your point gracefully after being interrupted.

nodes.title: Holding your own in a debate
nodes.description: Staying composed and articulate under direct challenge.

nodes.title: Presenting to an unfamiliar audience
nodes.description: Adjusting delivery confidently for a larger or unfamiliar audience.

nodes.title: Accepting and responding to criticism
nodes.description: Responding professionally and constructively to critical feedback.

nodes.title: Self-evaluating against B2 can-do statements
nodes.description: Reflecting on progress using B2-level concrete can-do statements.

nodes.title: Taking risks with complex language
nodes.description: Attempting more sophisticated structures even at the risk of a mistake.
